narrative: Jindal Stainless presented the sharpest ESG contrast in FY2023: it had the highest number of rank-one outcomes at 37, but also 37 rank-four outcomes. The company’s environmental position was structurally strong because of its smaller stainless-steel, scrap-linked and EAF-oriented footprint. It led the GHG and Scope 3 theme on Scope 1, Scope 2 and physical-output intensity, reporting 2.58 MtCO2e Scope 1, 0.74 MtCO2e Scope 2 and 2.1 tCO2e/tcs. It was also the clear air-emissions leader and the best absolute water and energy performer, with low total withdrawal, consumption and energy intensity. Jindal’s BRSR confirms a standalone boundary and a stainless-focused product base, with exports contributing about 15.49% of turnover, reinforcing its niche, higher-export structural profile. The weaknesses were mostly disclosure, scale and people-system related. It was last on net worth and turnover due to its smaller business model, last on employee gender diversity, weak on product-information coverage and poor on worker LTIFR. Waste was also mixed: lowest generation but highest total disposal. Governance indicators were thin, with very low sustainability capex and R&D alignment. Overall, FY2023 shows Jindal as the environmental intensity benchmark among the four, but with a less mature social, governance and product-responsibility disclosure platform.
